item.title: Install Avogadro v1.2
item.description: Avogadro is an advanced molecule editor and visualizer designed for cross-platform use in computational chemistry, molecular modeling, bioinformatics, materials science, and related areas. It offers flexible high quality rendering and a powerful plugin architecture. More details about Avogadro can be found [here]{https://avogadro.cc/}. Since Ubuntu 20.04 LTS release, the version of Avogadro in its official repos (focal/jammy) has changed from v1.2 to v1.95. However, the seemingly newer version does not offer enough functionality in terms of tweaking bond parameters, UFF optimization, and general ease of use.

item.title: Keep Ubuntu 20.04 (or it's derivatives) updated and clean
item.description: This also works for all debian based distributions. It is a good practice to keep our system updated and all the packages and libraries to their latest version. Specially in long-term support (LTS) versions such as Ubuntu 20.04 (Focal Fossa). This article provides a pretty straightforward way as to how to keep your system to its latest. Follow the link and copy the contents of this file. Open up your favourite text editor and paste the copied content.

item.title: DIIS: Python code for H2O using minimal STO-3G basis set
item.description: The theory for the DIIS method can be found in the Notes section, or directly click here. As a reference, a normal SCF procedure (without DIIS implementation) took around 38 iterations as compared to just 11 iterations with DIIS implementation (we will see in this example). The energy and density convergence criteria were same in both cases. Some one-electron, and two-electron integrals are required for the code to work and those files are made available here.

item.title: DIIS: A method to accelarate SCF convergence
item.description: Introduction Direct Inversion of the Iterative Subspace (DIIS) is a method first proposed by Pulay1 which accelarates the convergence of a normal SCF procedure. Method Iterative procedures can give us a set of trial vectors {$\mathbf{p}^i$}. Thus we can form a residual (error) term between two sucessive iterations. $$\mathbf{e}^i = \mathbf{p}^{i+1} - \mathbf{p}^i$$ The DIIS method assumes that the final solution can be extrapolated as the linear combination of the current subspace generated from previous iterations.

item.title: Kalsubai Peak: First Monsoon Trek in the Sahyadris
item.description: This is a brief account of my first trek in the Sahyadri range to the Kalsubai peak. Should you wish to skip reading the main content, please go to the end of the post where you will find key details about the trek. What a day to cherish! If I could summarize it in one line, it would be: When a 2815 feet plan fails, go for the 5400 feet one.

item.title: Contact
item.description: Address for correspondence D-225, Tata Institute of Fundamental Research, Dr. Homi Bhabha Road, Old Navy Nagar, Colaba, Mumbai - 400 005. Electronic Mail pulkit.joshi@tifr.res.in pulkitjoshi896@gmail.com

item.title: About
item.description: I am currently a PhD scholar in the Quantum Chemistry Group at Tata Institute of Fundamental Research. Some of my research interests are listed below. Apart from work, I enjoy playing table tennis, go for hikes, and treks. You can read about some of the interesting ones in the blogs section. I am also interested in learning programming languages like Python, C++, HTML5, CSS3, and Bash. Research Interests Exact theories for multireference systems (open or closed shell) Quantum chemical methods beyond the random phase approximation (RPA) within the density functional theory (DFT) framework.
